title: The Promise and the Machine
bullets: There is no neutral history of the Soviet Union: for seventy-four years the state lied about itself as policy, and its Cold War enemies produced their own distorted picture in the other direction.
Formally the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, 1922 to 1991, at its height the largest country on earth, about 8.6 million square miles spanning eleven time zones.
It was the world's first attempt to run a country on Marxism, the ideas of Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, worked out in the slums of industrial Britain, not agrarian Russia.
The promise was real and intoxicating: an end to landlords, tsars, and bosses; the machine was a one-party state with a political police and theorized terror from within weeks of taking power.

Robert Conquest's The Great Terror (1968) and The Harvest of Sorrow (1986) put the killings on a scale before any archive could be checked; the opened files vindicated his shape while revising numbers down.
The Soviet secret police was not a Bolshevik invention but the direct continuation of the Tsarist Okhrana, inherited from the Russian Empire and then expanded past anything the tsars attempted.
By 1917 Russia was a vast agrarian empire that had abolished serfdom only in 1861, ruled by a Romanov tsar, stumbling through the First World War with a disintegrating army.
Memorial, the Russian human-rights group founded in 1989 to document the repressions, built the fullest victim databases of all and was forcibly liquidated by the Russian state in 2021–2022.
Every major death toll in Soviet history is contested and the honest figures are ranges, not points, because the events were deliberately undocumented and weighted differently.
Two revolutions, eight months apart: the February Revolution overthrew the monarchy and Tsar Nicholas II abdicated, ending three hundred years of Romanov rule in under a week.

What replaced the tsar was dual power: the liberal Provisional Government, committed to staying in the world war, and the soviets, grassroots councils that held the actual loyalty of soldiers and workers.
The Bolsheviks, led by Lenin, were the disciplined vanguard wing of Russia's Marxist movement that had split from the moderate Mensheviks in a 1903 faction fight.
Lenin returned in April 1917 in a sealed German train and issued the April Theses: no support for the Provisional Government, no war, all power to the soviets, and 'Peace, Land, and Bread.'
The Kornilov affair backfired so badly the Provisional Government had to arm the workers and free jailed Bolsheviks, handing Lenin's party weapons and a majority in the Petrograd and Moscow soviets.

On the night of 24–25 October 1917 the October Revolution took the bridges and stations of Petrograd against almost no resistance; the Storming of the Winter Palace was a tense, mostly bloodless occupation.

The new Council of People's Commissars, or Sovnarkom, with Lenin as chairman, issued the Decree on Peace and the Decree on Land, the land program lifted wholesale from the rival Socialist Revolutionaries.

The Bolsheviks lost the November 1917 elections to the freely elected Constituent Assembly, then dissolved it by armed sailors after a single day, nine weeks after taking power, against the electorate's express wishes.

title: The Civil War
bullets: Holding the largest country on earth took three and a half years of warfare that killed far more Russians than the First World War and forged almost every feature of the later Soviet state.
The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in March 1918 surrendered Poland, the Baltic, Finland, Ukraine, and a third of the old empire's population to Germany to free the Bolsheviks to fight the civil war.

Reds against Whites was the spine, but the country was a chaos of armies: monarchist generals like Denikin and Kolchak, the Greens under the anarchist Nestor Makhno, and national independence movements everywhere.
The Czechoslovak Legion revolt seized the Trans-Siberian Railway and turned scattered resistance into a full-scale civil war, also triggering the murder of the Romanovs.

Britain, France, Japan, and the United States landed troops in the Allied intervention; militarily indecisive, politically a gift to Lenin and the source of the lasting Soviet conviction of capitalist encirclement.
Trotsky built the Red Army from scratch into millions, recruiting tsarist officers as 'military specialists' shadowed by political commissars with their families held as hostages for their loyalty.

The Cheka under Felix Dzerzhinsky, founded December 1917, ran the Red Terror, an announced policy of shooting class enemies by category; deaths range from tens of thousands to several hundred thousand.
On the night of 16–17 July 1918 Nicholas II, his wife, five children, and servants were shot in a Yekaterinburg cellar; the killing is fully documented, the precise authorization inferred.
War Communism, the forced requisition of grain by armed detachments, won the war and wrecked agriculture, leaving the regime more endangered in 1921 than at any point in the fighting.

title: The Tactical Retreat
bullets: In March 1921 the sailors of Kronstadt, the radical shock troops of 1917, rose against the Bolshevik government for free soviets and an end to the political monopoly; the Red Army stormed the fortress across the ice.

The Tambov rebellion, a peasant war south of Moscow, was suppressed with artillery, burned villages, mass hostage-taking, and one of the first documented uses of poison gas by a state against its own population.
Lenin's response at the Tenth Party Congress was the New Economic Policy, the NEP, a deliberate partial restoration of the market that revived the economy and produced the despised NEPmen.
The same congress passed the ban on factions, an emergency measure aimed at the Workers' Opposition and Alexandra Kollontai that strangled internal democracy and that Stalin inherited fully loaded.

The Russian famine of 1921–22 killed roughly one to five million; the regime publicly admitted it and accepted Herbert Hoover's American Relief Administration, which fed about ten million people a day.

On 30 December 1922 the Russian RSFSR, Ukraine, Belarus, and the Transcaucasian federation were joined into the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, with paper rights to secede that meant nothing for sixty-nine years.
Stalin became General Secretary in April 1922, the dullest senior post and the most powerful, controlling appointments, files, and personnel across the whole Party.

In Lenin's Testament he called explicitly for Stalin's removal as 'too rude'; the document was genuine, suppressed by the leadership, and not read to the Party until 1956, thirty-three years too late.

title: The Grey Blur Wins
bullets: When Lenin died in January 1924 almost no one bet on Stalin; the diarist Nikolai Sukhanov had called him a 'grey blur,' a line that stuck because it captured how thoroughly he was underestimated.
Stalin won by controlling the Party machinery rather than winning arguments, appointing loyalists who elected the congress delegates and positioning every rival as an illegal 'faction.'
A triumvirate of Stalin, Zinoviev, and Kamenev crushed Trotsky; then Stalin turned on Zinoviev and Kamenev, who joined Trotsky in a United Opposition that was broken and expelled by 1927.
Trotsky was deported from the USSR in 1929, beginning a wandering that ended in 1940 when a Soviet agent killed him in Mexico with an ice axe.
Stalin then adopted the radical economic line and destroyed Bukharin and the Right Opposition who defended the NEP; every famous name of 1917 except Stalin was politically destroyed by Stalin.
Socialism in One Country, his doctrine that the USSR could build socialism alone, beat Trotsky's Permanent Revolution and was the political weapon and the blueprint for the bloodiest decade.
By his 1929 fiftieth-birthday year the cult of personality opened, presenting Stalin as the infallible Vozhd, the living continuation of Lenin.
1929 was the Great Break, the velikii perelom: the NEP torn up, control of the Party unchallengeable, and the campaign against the peasantry beginning the same month as the worship.

Whether Stalinism was the betrayal of the revolution or its fulfilment is the single most contested argument in Soviet historiography, and an honest account names all three positions rather than picking one.

title: The War on the Village
bullets: Between 1929 and 1933 the Soviet state went to war against its own peasantry to seize the food supply and break the village as an independent social world.

The kulak, in theory a rich exploiting peasant, became in practice any peasant who resisted, designated locally by quota with no hearing and no appeal, including the invented 'kulak helper,' the podkulachnik.
Collectivization forced households into kolkhozy; dekulakization deported roughly 1.8 million people to special settlements, with credible estimates of well over a million dead.
Peasants slaughtered and ate their own livestock, collapsing the herds by roughly half and crippling the new collective farms at the worst possible moment.
Stalin's article 'Dizzy with Success' in March 1930 blamed the chaos on overzealous local officials carrying out his own orders, propaganda cover that vented peasant fury onto subordinates.
The 1932–33 famine across Ukraine, the Kuban, the Volga, and Kazakhstan killed roughly 5.5 to 7 million; it was man-made, caused by brutal procurement, blockaded regions, and the 'law of spikelets.'
The Holodomor, the Ukrainian catastrophe of roughly 3.5 to 5 million dead, is the subject of a genuine, unsettled dispute over whether it was a genocide, a live political weapon today.
The Kazakh famine of 1930–33, the Asharshylyk, killed an estimated 1.5 million, a third or more of the entire Kazakh people, the highest proportional death rate and barely known in the West.
By 1933 the war on the village was won, the independent peasantry destroyed, at a price across the whole USSR of roughly six to eight million dead.

title: The Furnace
bullets: In roughly a single decade an overwhelmingly agrarian country was dragged at terrible cost into being the world's second industrial power, in time to survive a war that would have annihilated the agrarian version.
The First Five-Year Plan, launched 1928, made Gosplan set output targets for every enterprise by administrative order, building falsification into the system's nervous system from the start.
Magnitogorsk, a steel city built on bare Ural steppe modeled on Gary, Indiana, was raised by volunteers, conscripted peasants, and deported kulaks living in dugouts through the winter.
Dnieprostroi, the Dnieper Hydroelectric Dam in Ukraine completed 1932, was for a time the largest in Europe, blown up by the retreating Red Army in 1941 rather than left to the Germans.
Soviet heavy-industry output grew at rates that, during the Great Depression, convinced significant numbers of Western intellectuals that planning worked.

The Soviet First Five-Year Plans demolished even Japan's record, compressing a comparable transformation into roughly a decade with coercion no industrializing society had deliberately chosen in so concentrated a form.
The cult of the worker had a face in Alexei Stakhanov, the coal miner credited with cutting many times his quota in one staged night; the Stakhanovite movement ratcheted up norms for everyone else.
The Likbez literacy campaign took Soviet literacy from a minority to near-universal in a generation, a real and enormous achievement experienced as liberation at the same time millions were deported.

The furnace built the industrial base that out-produced Germany in tanks by 1943 and was copied by Nehru's India, but produced a model good only at crash mobilization and structurally bad at everything else.

title: The Vanishing
bullets: The Gulag, the Main Administration of Camps, expanded by Stalin from around 1929 as both a punishment system and an explicit economic one, supplying prison labor for Kolyma's gold and Norilsk's nickel.

Roughly 18 million people passed through the Gulag over its full Stalin-era existence, the camp population peaking near two and a half million, recorded camp deaths on the order of 1.6 to 1.7 million.

The assassination of Sergei Kirov, the popular Leningrad Party boss, on 1 December 1934 was the pretext for the Terror; that Stalin ordered the murder is a credible hypothesis, not an established fact.
The Great Terror, the Yezhovshchina under Nikolai Yezhov, ran principally 1936 to 1938; Yezhov was himself arrested in 1939 and shot in 1940, the executioner consumed by the machine.

The Moscow show trials made the surviving Old Bolsheviks, Zinoviev, Kamenev, and Bukharin, confess to impossible conspiracies and be shot, rewriting the revolution so every founder but Stalin was a traitor.
The purge of the Red Army shot or imprisoned nearly the entire top officer corps including Marshal Tukhachevsky, a self-inflicted decapitation the Wehrmacht exploited savagely in 1941.
The hidden mass operations, above all Order No. 00447, set numerical execution quotas by province and produced roughly 1.5 to 1.7 million arrests and about 681,000 recorded executions in two years.

Stalin and other Politburo members personally signed the rasstrelnye spiski, death lists running to thousands of names that physically survive in the Party archive, so this is documentation, not inference.
It ran on hundreds of thousands of denunciations by ordinary citizens; the ordinary participation is the genuinely disturbing finding a simple 'a tyrant did it' story misses.

title: The New Soviet Man
bullets: The USSR was not only a coercive machine but a project to manufacture the New Soviet Man, collectivist, atheist, literate, free of the old loyalties of religion, nation, and family.

From the late 1920s the assault on the Russian Orthodox Church closed thousands of churches and mosques and shot or camped tens of thousands of clergy; the League of Militant Godless ran the propaganda.
The Cathedral of Christ the Saviour was dynamited in Moscow in 1931 for a Palace of the Soviets that was never built; the pit became a swimming pool and the cathedral was rebuilt after 1991.

Early Bolshevik policy on women was among the most radical on earth, legalizing divorce and abortion under Alexandra Kollontai's Zhenotdel, then reversed by Stalin, who recriminalized abortion in 1936.

Early nationalities policy was korenizatsiya, deliberately the opposite of Russification, the world's first 'affirmative action empire,' reversed under Stalin into purges and wartime deportation of whole nations.
Socialist Realism became the single compulsory artistic doctrine in 1932–34, with Maxim Gorky as figurehead of the all-controlling Union of Soviet Writers.

Mayakovsky shot himself in 1930; Osip Mandelstam died in a transit camp in 1938 for an epigram mocking Stalin; Anna Akhmatova composed Requiem in the prison queues; Shostakovich lived with a packed suitcase.
The Pioneers and the Komsomol were a single escalator giving millions from the bottom of the old order a structured route upward into education, careers, and the Party.
The Semashko system built free universal healthcare across a vast poor country with real gains in life expectancy, the liberation and the suffocation the same program.

title: The Pact
bullets: In August 1939 the self-proclaimed enemy of fascism signed a friendship treaty with Nazi Germany and jointly invaded and partitioned Poland with it.

The Comintern, founded 1919 as the general staff of world revolution, funded and ordered foreign Communist parties; its 'social fascist' line split the German left and eased Hitler's path to power in 1933.
The Popular Front from 1935 reversed it; the test case was Spain, where the USSR was the only major power to send the Republic substantial aid and the Comintern organized the International Brigades.
At Khalkhin Gol in 1939 Soviet forces under Georgy Zhukov crushed a Japanese army, pushing Japanese strategy south toward the Pacific and later freeing Stalin's Siberian divisions to save Moscow.
On 23 August 1939 Molotov and Ribbentrop signed the Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact with a Secret Protocol dividing Eastern Europe, an existence the USSR denied for fifty years until 1989.
On 17 September 1939 the USSR invaded Poland from the east; whether the pact was a defensible buy for time or an enabling crime is a genuine, politically live, unresolved argument.
The Baltic states, independent since 1918, were occupied and annexed in 1940; the refusal ever to accept it is what makes them the first to break away fifty years later.
In the Katyn massacre of spring 1940, on a Politburo order signed by Stalin, roughly 22,000 captured Polish officers and elite were shot; the USSR blamed the Germans for fifty years until 1990–92.
The Winter War against Finland from November 1939 was a humiliation that advertised to Hitler that the Soviet military was a hollow giant after the purge had shot its officer corps.

title: The Catastrophe
bullets: On 22 June 1941 Operation Barbarossa, roughly three million Axis troops, crossed into the USSR; the war was designed on the German side as a war of racial annihilation, not a conventional campaign.
Stalin had dismissed a flood of intelligence warnings, forbidding his commanders to ready forces; he did not address the nation for eleven days and briefly withdrew to his dacha.
In the first weeks the Germans destroyed Soviet armies in clean sweeps; at the Kiev encirclement in September roughly half a million or more troops were killed or captured.

The Germans captured around five to five and a half million Soviet soldiers and deliberately starved them; roughly three million died in captivity, one of the largest least-remembered mass killings of the war.
The evacuation of industry physically moved over 1,500 entire factories a thousand miles east, the brutal command economy's one supreme competence saving the state that built it.
The siege of Leningrad lasted nearly 900 days; the diarist Tanya Savicheva recorded her family's deaths one by one, and civilian deaths ran from roughly 800,000 to over a million.
The 'Road of Life' across frozen Lake Ladoga kept Leningrad barely alive under German artillery fire, many trucks breaking through the ice.

At the Battle of Moscow Zhukov, the victor of Khalkhin Gol, held the line and launched a December counteroffensive, the first significant German defeat of the war, aided by Sorge's intelligence and the winter.

title: The Turn
bullets: Something in the range of three-quarters to four-fifths of German military deaths in the Second World War occurred on the Eastern Front; the war against Nazi Germany was overwhelmingly decided in the East.
The Battle of Stalingrad, August 1942 to February 1943, became the most lethal battle in human history; Order No. 227, 'Not One Step Back,' created penal battalions and blocking detachments.
Operation Uranus trapped the entire German Sixth Army, about 250,000 men; roughly 90,000 surrendered and only some five or six thousand survived the camps to return home.
Kursk in July 1943, the largest tank battle in history, broke Germany's last great offensive in the East; Operation Bagration in 1944 destroyed Army Group Centre, far less remembered in the West.

As the Red Army advanced its troops committed mass rape and pillage against German and 'liberated' civilians, documented, suppressed for decades, and named here because this book launders no state's crimes.
The Red Army took Berlin in April–May 1945; the famous Reichstag flag-raising photograph, like the storming of the Winter Palace, was a staged and edited image.
Soviet citizens who died as a result of the war is conventionally given as about 27 million, with serious estimates running roughly 25 to 30 million; about two-thirds were civilians.
The war became the founding myth that retroactively redeemed the camps and the famine, the most powerful and most politically exploited element of Soviet memory in Putin's Russia.
Returning Soviet prisoners of war were treated as 'contaminated,' funneled through filtration camps and in large numbers sent to the Gulag for the crime of having been captured.

title: The Iron Curtain
bullets: The USSR emerged from the war devastated and triumphant, an estimated 70,000 destroyed villages and 27 million dead, yet its army standing in the heart of Europe and its prestige at its peak.
The descent into the Cold War was fast and the blame genuinely contested; the unresolvable knot throughout the Tehran, Yalta, and Potsdam summits was who would govern Poland.
Between 1945 and 1948 Communist parties took over Eastern Europe by what Mátyás Rákosi called 'salami tactics,' and Churchill's Iron Curtain hardened into a fortified frontier.
COMECON (1949) and the Warsaw Pact (1955) bound the satellites to Moscow; the Berlin Blockade was beaten by the Western Berlin Airlift and Germany split in 1949.
In 1949 the USSR detonated its first atomic bomb, accelerated by espionage including Klaus Fuchs, ending the U.S. nuclear monopoly and starting the arms race.
Yugoslavia's Tito refused Moscow's orders and survived the Tito–Stalin split of 1948 as a defiant independent Communist state, proving the monolith had seams.
The Zhdanovshchina tightened the ideological screws; the agronomist Trofim Lysenko, given dictatorial power over biology, crippled it for a generation and the botanist Vavilov starved in prison.

The repression turned anti-Semitic: the Jewish Anti-Fascist Committee was destroyed, the Leningrad Affair shot the city's leadership, and the Doctors' Plot was preparing a mass deportation of Soviet Jews.
Stalin suffered a stroke on the night of 1 March 1953, lay for hours because his guards were too frightened to enter, and died on 5 March 1953.

title: The Thaw
bullets: The succession was a frightened collective affair because Stalin had killed every obvious heir; the immediate menace was Lavrenty Beria, the secret-police chief who controlled the files on all of them.
Zhukov and armed officers physically arrested Beria at a Kremlin table in summer 1953; he was tried in secret and shot, the last time a Soviet leadership struggle was settled by execution.
Nikita Khrushchev, an earthy former metalworker who had himself signed Terror execution lists, became the unlikely agent of de-Stalinization.
On the night of 24–25 February 1956 Khrushchev's Secret Speech denounced Stalin's crimes to the Party elite, carefully bounded to attack the architect while declaring the foundations sound.
The speech reached Western intelligence within months and shattered permanently the infallibility on which the whole system's authority rested.
Millions were released from the Gulag and special settlements and the camp system was largely dismantled; rehabilitation was chaotic and bitter, often only for Party members.

In 1962, with Khrushchev's authorization, Novy Mir under Tvardovsky published Solzhenitsyn's One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich, the first time the Gulag was named in a sanctioned Soviet publication.

The Polish October produced a tolerated partial liberalization, but when Hungary announced it would leave the Warsaw Pact the USSR invaded in November 1956, killing around 2,500–3,000 and later executing Imre Nagy.
The message of 1956 was precise: internal reform within the system perhaps, departure from the bloc never; the man who gave the Secret Speech also sent the tanks into Budapest.

title: The Rocket and the Boot
bullets: The Khrushchev decade was the only period the USSR genuinely seemed to be winning the future, putting the first object, animal, man, and woman into space.
On 4 October 1957 Sputnik produced something close to panic in the United States, prompting the creation of NASA and a surge of federal science money within a year.
On 12 April 1961 Yuri Gagarin made a single 108-minute orbit that made the USSR for one moment the unambiguous leader of human civilization's outward step.
The hidden Chief Designer was Sergei Korolev, a brilliant engineer who had himself been a tortured Gulag prisoner on Kolyma, the most compact statement of the whole book in one biography.
The USSR could not match the American Moon landing because its N1 Moon rocket failed on all four test launches, then classified the entire program and denied it had ever tried.
At Novocherkassk in 1962 troops fired on striking workers, killing on the order of two dozen, an event so completely suppressed it was unknown until the 1990s.
In August 1961 the regime sealed the city overnight with the Berlin Wall, the most literal monument to a state that had to imprison its own citizens to keep them.
The Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 was the closest the world has come to deliberate nuclear war, ended by a negotiated climb-down and followed by the 1963 Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.

The Sino-Soviet split disintegrated the alliance with Mao's China into open rivalry, and the Virgin Lands Campaign failed; in October 1964 the Party voted Khrushchev out and, crucially, did not shoot him.

title: Developed Socialism
bullets: The eighteen Brezhnev years that almost no dramatic event defines are precisely their nature: the USSR became calm, predictable, a true superpower, and slowly, terminally ill.

Brezhnev's 'stability of cadres' consolidated the nomenklatura, the confidential lists controlling every position of consequence, producing a self-perpetuating ruling caste with its own closed economy of privilege.
The Kosygin reform of 1965 tried to give enterprises autonomy and use profit as a measure; its failure was the hinge of the era, strangled by the bureaucracy and political fear.
The Siberian oil and gas discoveries and the 1973 oil-price explosion flooded the USSR with hard currency that papered over the failed reform and postponed the structural reckoning for a decade.
For most citizens the Brezhnev era was the most materially secure life Russians had ever had, and a deadening, cynical, shortage-ridden grayness of the deficit and blat; both are true.

The Prague Spring's 'socialism with a human face' was crushed by Warsaw Pact tanks in August 1968, producing the Brezhnev Doctrine and shattering Soviet credibility among Western Communists into Eurocommunism.
Détente brought SALT I (1972), the Apollo–Soyuz docking (1975), and the Helsinki Accords (1975), whose human-rights clause Moscow regarded as boilerplate and that turned out to be a time bomb.
The 1977 Brezhnev Constitution guaranteed an extensive catalogue of rights while Article 6 constitutionally enshrined the Party's 'leading role' that made them all unenforceable.
Brezhnev's last years were a grim spectacle of a visibly senile leader and a sclerotic Politburo governing a country whose problems no one would name.

title: The Cracks
bullets: The Soviet dissident movement never numbered more than a few thousand active people in a country of 250 million and never threatened the state's power directly.
Samizdat was the practice of retyping banned texts on carbon paper, a fragile underground photocopier made of human beings; there was also tamizdat and the magnitizdat songs of Vladimir Vysotsky.
The 1965–66 Sinyavsky–Daniel trial, prosecuting writers for the content of their fiction, backfired and grew the human-rights movement and the long-running samizdat Chronicle of Current Events.
After the 1975 Helsinki Accords the Helsinki Groups used the regime's own signature to demand the rights on the page, the regime's trap closing on itself.
Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn authorized the publication abroad of The Gulag Archipelago in 1973 and was stripped of citizenship and deported in 1974.
Andrei Sakharov, the USSR's most celebrated physicist and father of its hydrogen bomb, turned internal critic, won the 1975 Nobel Peace Prize, and was exiled without trial to Gorky in 1980.
Punitive psychiatry committed dissidents to special hospitals with a constructed diagnosis of 'sluggish schizophrenia'; the case of General Pyotr Grigorenko put it before the world.
The refuseniks, Soviet Jews refused permission to emigrate then punished for asking, became an international campaign exposing a regime that had to forbid its citizens to leave.
The nationalities, suppressed grievances over the Holodomor, the deportations, and the Baltic annexation, were the fault line the dissidents partly carried and that opens all the way at the end.

title: The Wall the Economy Hit
bullets: Between 1979 and 1985 the USSR ran into the wall: an unwinnable war, a ruinous arms race, dying leaders, and an economy whose hidden failure was exposed when the oil price hiding it fell.

The invasion of Afghanistan in December 1979 became a nine-year counterinsurgency, the USSR's Vietnam, with roughly 15,000 Soviet dead and Afghan dead estimated from several hundred thousand to two million.
Reagan's military buildup and the 1983 Strategic Defense Initiative, 'Star Wars,' frightened a Soviet leadership that had watched the U.S. pull decisively ahead in computing and microelectronics.

In September 1983 a Soviet interceptor shot down Korean Air Lines Flight 007, killing all 269 aboard; the NATO exercise Able Archer 83 was read by parts of the leadership as possible cover for a first strike.
Brezhnev died in November 1982, Andropov in February 1984, Chernenko in March 1985: three Soviet leaders died in twenty-eight months.
The thing that actually killed the USSR was the command economy hitting the terminus Chapter 7 predicted, masked through the 1970s by oil and exposed when the price collapsed in 1986.
In March 1985 the exhausted Politburo deliberately chose its youngest serious member, the 54-year-old reformer Mikhail Gorbachev, in the hope he would fix what the old men could not.
The leadership that chose Gorbachev to save the system had just made the decision that ended it.

title: The Repairman
bullets: Mikhail Gorbachev was a committed Communist trying to save and renew socialism who set out to repair the machine and discovered it could not be repaired without exposing the foundations.
His anti-alcohol campaign attacked a genuine catastrophe, improving some health figures while devastating the state budget and making the government instantly unpopular.
Perestroika, the attempt to reform the economy, largely failed, leaving the economy stranded between two systems with worsening shortages and emptier shelves than anything in the stagnation.
Glasnost became a genuine, accelerating freedom of speech the regime lost the ability to limit, and what people spoke about above all was the suppressed past.
Chernobyl in April 1986 was the worst nuclear accident in history; the cover-up broke from abroad when Swedish workers detected the fallout, convincing Gorbachev secrecy itself was fatal.
Memorial was founded in 1989 to document the repressions and the state began admitting its own archival crimes, including finally releasing the Katyn order.
Gorbachev abandoned the Brezhnev Doctrine, and in the Revolutions of 1989 every Communist government in Eastern Europe fell, the Berlin Wall opened on 9 November 1989, and Germany reunified in 1990.
On 23 August 1989, the fiftieth anniversary of the pact, close to two million people linked hands across about 420 miles in the Baltic Way demanding the secret protocol be acknowledged and undone.
The 1989 elections to the Congress of People's Deputies put Sakharov, recalled from exile, and Boris Yeltsin on the national stage, and by 1990 the Party had given up its constitutional monopoly.

title: The End
bullets: The largest country on earth, a nuclear-armed superpower, ceased to exist in December 1991 without a war, without an invasion, and with almost no one killed.

The hardliners moved on 19 August 1991 to stop the new Union Treaty; the August Coup collapsed in three days because its leaders were the gerontocracy's last dregs and the army would not fire on civilians.
Boris Yeltsin climbed onto a tank outside the Russian White House to denounce the coup, the single defining image of the end of Soviet Communism.
Yeltsin banned the Communist Party on Russian soil and the republics declared independence one after another.
On 1 December 1991 Ukrainians voted for full independence by over 90 percent, carrying even the heavily Russian east; a USSR without Ukraine was not a viable union.
On 8 December 1991 the leaders of Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus signed the Belovezha Accords declaring the USSR ceasing to exist, and Yeltsin phoned the American president before telling Gorbachev.

On 25 December 1991 Gorbachev resigned on television and the red flag was lowered from the Kremlin for the last time, in a quiet so complete it is itself one of the most astonishing facts in the story.
The legacy is fiercely fought over: one reading holds the USSR primarily a catastrophe, another holds it industrialized a backward country, defeated fascism, and put humanity into space.
The fifteen successor states did utterly different things with one inheritance, from the EU democracies of the Baltics to a Ukraine whose violent separation is the largest war in Europe since 1945.
